name: Puget Sound
description:
Puget Sound is the largest estuary in the Pacific Northwest and one of the most productive marine ecosystems on Earth. Stretching over 1,332 miles of shoreline across ten Washington counties, this deep saltwater inlet connects to the Pacific Ocean through the Strait of Juan de Fuca and offers world-class fishing from the urban waterfront of Seattle to the remote inlets of the Olympic Peninsula. Its depths reach an impressive 930 feet, creating a complex underwater landscape of channels, basins, and rocky reefs that support an extraordinary diversity of marine life.

Salmon fishing defines the Puget Sound experience, with Chinook, Coho, and Pink Salmon drawing thousands of anglers each season from July through October. Bottom fishing for Lingcod and Rockfish is equally rewarding, particularly around underwater structure and rocky points. Halibut openers bring intense excitement each spring, and the Sound's Dungeness Crab and Spot Prawn fisheries are among the finest recreational shellfish opportunities in the nation.

Boat launches are plentiful throughout the Sound, with major facilities in Seattle, Tacoma, Olympia, Bremerton, Everett, and Bellingham providing easy access to both protected waters and open channels. Whether you are trolling for salmon off Point No Point, jigging for Lingcod near Deception Pass, or dropping crab pots in Hood Canal, Puget Sound delivers an unmatched saltwater fishing experience in a breathtaking natural setting.

name: San Juan Islands
description:
The San Juan Islands archipelago comprises 172 named islands scattered across the northernmost waters of Washington State, creating one of the most spectacular saltwater fishing destinations on the West Coast. The deep channels between the islands plunge to 600 feet and funnel nutrient-rich currents from the Pacific Ocean, supporting a thriving marine food web that attracts everything from baitfish to orca whales. This pristine island chain in San Juan County is equally famous for wildlife viewing and world-class angling.

Chinook Salmon fishing around the San Juan Islands is legendary, with trophy-class kings running through the channels from spring through fall. Lingcod and Rockfish inhabit the countless rocky reefs and ledges surrounding the islands, offering outstanding bottom fishing year-round. Halibut fishing in the deeper channels produces impressive flatfish, while Cabezon and Greenling provide exciting action for anglers working nearshore structure and kelp beds.

Friday Harbor on San Juan Island serves as the primary hub for visiting anglers, with charter services, marinas, and boat launches readily available. Orcas Island and Lopez Island also offer launch facilities and moorage. The combination of protected island waters, deep-channel fishing, and the chance to spot resident orca pods, bald eagles, and harbor seals makes the San Juan Islands a truly unforgettable fishing destination in the Pacific Northwest.

name: Strait of Juan de Fuca
description:
The Strait of Juan de Fuca is the primary ocean gateway connecting the Pacific to Puget Sound, stretching along the northern coast of the Olympic Peninsula between Washington State and Vancouver Island, British Columbia. With depths reaching 720 feet and powerful tidal exchanges bringing cold, nutrient-rich ocean water through its channels, the Strait supports some of the most productive saltwater fishing in the Pacific Northwest. Clallam and Jefferson counties share this remarkable waterway, which offers anglers direct access to ocean-run fish without venturing onto the open Pacific.

Chinook and Coho Salmon fishing in the Strait is outstanding from late spring through early fall, as massive runs of ocean-bright fish funnel through on their way to rivers throughout the region. Halibut fishing is a major draw, with productive grounds located within easy reach of ports like Sekiu, Neah Bay, and Port Angeles. Lingcod and Rockfish round out the bottom fishing opportunities, with rocky reefs and underwater pinnacles holding large populations of these hard-fighting species.

Neah Bay at the western tip of the Strait is renowned as one of Washington's premier fishing ports, offering access to both Strait and open-ocean waters. Port Angeles provides a central launch point with full marina services, while Port Townsend and Sequim offer additional access on the eastern end. The dramatic backdrop of the Olympic Mountains and the Strait's ever-changing marine weather create a fishing experience that is as visually stunning as it is rewarding.

name: Willapa Bay
description:
Willapa Bay is widely recognized as one of the cleanest and most pristine estuaries in the United States, spanning 120 miles of shoreline along the southwestern Washington coast in Pacific County. This shallow coastal bay, with a maximum depth of just 25 feet, is fed by the Willapa, Naselle, and North rivers, creating a rich mixing zone of fresh and salt water that supports extraordinary populations of shellfish, salmon, and sturgeon. The bay produces nearly one-sixth of all oysters harvested in the United States, earning it a reputation as one of the Pacific Northwest's great natural treasures.

Chinook Salmon fishing in Willapa Bay draws anglers throughout the summer and fall, with fish staging in the bay before ascending tributary rivers. Sturgeon fishing is a time-honored tradition in the deeper channels, where both white and green sturgeon can be targeted. Dungeness Crab are abundant in the bay's eelgrass beds, and the recreational oyster harvest is a unique experience found in few other locations. Steelhead enter the bay's tributaries during the winter months, adding another dimension to the angling calendar.

The communities of Long Beach, Raymond, and South Bend provide boat launch access and services for visiting anglers. South Bend, the county seat, bills itself as the "Oyster Capital of the World" and serves as a gateway to the bay's best fishing grounds. The bay's shallow, protected waters are well-suited for smaller boats, and its relatively uncrowded conditions offer a peaceful alternative to the busier fishing destinations found elsewhere along the Washington coast.

name: Grays Harbor
description:
Grays Harbor is a major coastal estuary on Washington's central coast, offering 80 miles of shoreline and direct access to both protected bay waters and the open Pacific Ocean. Located in Grays Harbor County, this dynamic waterway is fed by the Chehalis, Humptulips, and Wishkah rivers, creating a productive ecosystem where freshwater and saltwater species converge. The harbor's maximum depth of 30 feet and its expansive tidal flats make it an exceptionally rich habitat for salmon, crab, and a wide variety of marine fish.

The fishing port of Westport, situated at the harbor's southern entrance, is one of Washington's most important sportfishing hubs, providing access to offshore Halibut grounds, coastal Lingcod reefs, and the harbor's prolific Chinook and Coho Salmon runs. Dungeness Crab fishing is outstanding throughout the bay and along the adjacent coast, and Sturgeon provide a challenging target in the harbor's deeper channels. The combination of bay, jetty, and offshore fishing makes Grays Harbor one of the most versatile angling destinations in the state.

Ocean Shores on the north side and Westport on the south side bracket the harbor entrance, each offering boat launches, marinas, and full visitor services. The inland communities of Aberdeen and Hoquiam serve as gateways to the upper harbor and its tributary rivers. Whether you are trolling for salmon in the harbor mouth, crabbing on the tidal flats, or making an offshore run for Halibut and Lingcod, Grays Harbor delivers a complete coastal fishing experience.

name: Lake Chelan
description:
Lake Chelan is the third deepest lake in the United States, plunging to an astounding 1,486 feet in its glacially carved basin in the heart of the North Cascades. This 55-mile-long fjord-like lake stretches from the sun-drenched orchards and vineyards near the town of Chelan into the rugged wilderness of the Lake Chelan-Sawtooth Wilderness Area, where the remote village of Stehekin can only be reached by boat, float plane, or trail. With 32,600 acres of crystal-clear water and 82 miles of shoreline, Lake Chelan is one of Washington's most iconic natural landmarks.

Kokanee Salmon are the bread-and-butter fishery on Lake Chelan, with strong runs providing fast action for trollers from late spring through fall. Lake Trout inhabit the deep, cold waters and can reach impressive sizes, with fish exceeding 20 pounds taken regularly by anglers using downriggers and deep-trolling techniques. Rainbow Trout and landlocked Chinook Salmon add to the coldwater fishing opportunities, while Smallmouth Bass provide excellent warm-water action in the shallower bays near Chelan and Manson. Burbot, an unusual freshwater cod, are targeted through the ice in winter.

The communities of Chelan and Manson at the lake's southeastern end offer multiple boat launches, marinas, and full visitor services including lodging, restaurants, and tackle shops. The lake's dramatic depth and clear water create unique fishing challenges, but the reward is a day on one of the most visually spectacular lakes in North America, surrounded by towering peaks, alpine forests, and a wilderness that has changed little in centuries.

name: Moses Lake
description:
Moses Lake is a sprawling 6,800-acre warm-water fishery in the heart of Washington's Columbia Basin, offering 120 miles of intricate shoreline across its many arms, bays, and islands. Located in Grant County near the city of Moses Lake, this shallow desert lake reaches a maximum depth of 38 feet and is fed by irrigation return flows from the Columbia Basin Project, which keep water levels stable and nutrient levels high throughout the growing season. The result is a remarkably productive fishery that supports large populations of multiple game fish species.

Walleye fishing on Moses Lake has become increasingly popular, with fish in the 2- to 6-pound range available throughout the lake's deeper channels and along windswept points. Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass thrive in the lake's extensive weed beds, submerged rock piles, and riprap shorelines, making it one of eastern Washington's best bass destinations. Yellow Perch, Bluegill, and Crappie provide outstanding panfish action for families and shore anglers, while stocked Rainbow Trout offer additional opportunity during the cooler months.

The city of Moses Lake sits directly on the lake's shores, providing convenient access to multiple public boat launches, parks, and fishing docks. The lake's warm summer temperatures, sheltered coves, and abundant fish populations make it an ideal destination for anglers of all skill levels. Its location in the sunny Columbia Basin means favorable weather conditions from spring through fall, with over 300 days of sunshine annually creating an extended fishing season that few Washington lakes can match.

name: Banks Lake
description:
Banks Lake is a 27,000-acre reservoir created by pumping water from the Columbia River behind Grand Coulee Dam into the ancient Grand Coulee, a massive glacial channel carved during the Ice Age floods. Stretching 27 miles between Coulee City and Electric City in Grant and Douglas counties, Banks Lake fills a dramatic basalt-walled canyon that reaches 172 feet deep and serves as the primary equalizing reservoir for the Columbia Basin Irrigation Project. The lake's unique geology, stable water levels, and diverse habitat make it one of Washington's premier multi-species fisheries.

Walleye are the star attraction at Banks Lake, with consistent catches of fish in the 15- to 22-inch range and legitimate trophy potential for fish exceeding 10 pounds. Smallmouth Bass fishing is outstanding along the lake's extensive basalt shorelines and rocky points, with summertime action rivaling the best bass lakes in the state. Lake Trout inhabit the deepest portions of the reservoir and can reach impressive sizes, while Kokanee provide exciting light-tackle trolling opportunities. Yellow Perch and Burbot round out the diverse fishery and are especially popular with winter anglers.

Steamboat Rock State Park, located on a dramatic basalt mesa jutting into the lake, is the centerpiece of the Banks Lake recreation area and offers excellent boat launch facilities. Additional launches at Coulee City, Electric City, and Osborn Bay provide access to all sections of the reservoir. The surrounding landscape of towering basalt cliffs, sagebrush-covered plateaus, and big open skies creates a uniquely beautiful setting for a fishing trip in the heart of Washington's inland empire.

name: Lake Roosevelt
description:
Lake Roosevelt is one of the largest reservoirs in the United States, stretching 130 miles behind Grand Coulee Dam along the Columbia River through the rugged landscape of northeastern Washington. Encompassing 82,000 acres and 600 miles of shoreline across five counties, this massive impoundment is managed as the Lake Roosevelt National Recreation Area by the National Park Service. The reservoir's sheer size, remote character, and diverse fishery make it a destination unlike any other in the Pacific Northwest.

Walleye fishing on Lake Roosevelt is among the best in the western United States, with productive fishing from spring through fall along the reservoir's countless points, bays, and creek inflows. Rainbow Trout are stocked in large numbers by the Spokane Tribe and state fisheries agencies, creating a popular put-and-take fishery that produces consistent catches of pan-sized fish and occasional holdovers exceeding 5 pounds. Kokanee provide exciting summer trolling, and Smallmouth Bass have established strong populations in the warmer, shallower arms of the reservoir. White Sturgeon inhabit the deeper pools and represent a unique catch-and-release opportunity.

Boat launch facilities are scattered along the reservoir's length, with major access points at Kettle Falls, Seven Bays, Fort Spokane, Hunters, and Spring Canyon. The communities along the lake are small and rural, lending a wilderness character that is increasingly rare on western reservoirs. Camping is available at numerous National Park Service campgrounds along the shore, making Lake Roosevelt an ideal destination for multi-day fishing trips that combine outstanding angling with a true backcountry experience.

name: Potholes Reservoir
description:
Potholes Reservoir is a 28,200-acre impoundment in the heart of Washington's Columbia Basin, created where irrigation wastewater from the Columbia Basin Project collects in a vast network of sand dunes, lava flows, and natural depressions. Located in Grant and Adams counties with 100 miles of shoreline, the reservoir is famous for its unique "sand dunes" area — a labyrinth of small islands, channels, and hidden coves formed by partially submerged sand dunes that create some of the most interesting and productive fishing structure in the state.

Walleye are the premier game fish at Potholes Reservoir, with the open-water main body of the lake producing excellent catches of fish averaging 14 to 20 inches, along with consistent trophy opportunities. Largemouth Bass fishing in the sand dunes area is exceptional, with dense weed growth and countless ambush points holding bass throughout the warmer months. Smallmouth Bass patrol the rocky dam face and riprap shorelines, while Crappie, Bluegill, and Yellow Perch offer outstanding panfish action that draws families from across the region. Rainbow Trout are stocked seasonally and provide additional variety.

Mardon Resort and the state park at the southern end of the reservoir offer well-maintained boat launches and full services for visiting anglers. The sand dunes area on the reservoir's southern end is best explored by smaller boats or kayaks, while the open main body accommodates larger vessels. The reservoir's location in the sunny, arid Columbia Basin ensures warm summers and an extended fishing season, making Potholes one of eastern Washington's most popular and productive year-round fishing destinations.

name: Lake Washington
description:
Lake Washington is the second largest natural lake in Washington State and the centerpiece of the Seattle metropolitan area, stretching 22 miles between the cities of Seattle and Bellevue in King County. With 22,138 acres of water, 65 miles of shoreline, and a maximum depth of 214 feet, this urban lake is connected to Puget Sound through the Lake Washington Ship Canal and Ballard Locks. Despite its heavily developed shoreline and urban setting, Lake Washington supports a remarkably healthy and diverse fishery that provides outstanding angling within minutes of downtown Seattle.

Smallmouth Bass are the lake's marquee warm-water species, with rocky shorelines, submerged rubble, and dock pilings holding strong populations of fish that regularly exceed 4 pounds. Largemouth Bass thrive in the weedy bays and around residential docks, while Yellow Perch provide reliable panfish action year-round. Cutthroat Trout cruise the lake's nearshore waters and can be targeted with light tackle, and the lake hosts one of the largest Sockeye Salmon runs in the lower 48 states, with hundreds of thousands of fish transiting through the Ship Canal each summer.

Boat launches are available at numerous parks along the lakeshore, including public facilities in Seattle, Bellevue, Kirkland, Kenmore, and Renton. The lake is easily accessible by car from anywhere in the greater Seattle area, and its calm, protected waters make it suitable for boats of all sizes. Whether you are casting for bass from a kayak, trolling for trout with a downrigger, or watching the Sockeye run from the Ballard Locks fish ladder, Lake Washington offers a uniquely urban fishing experience against the backdrop of the Cascade Range and Olympic Mountains.

name: Lake Sammamish
description:
Lake Sammamish is a scenic 4,897-acre lake nestled in the foothills east of Seattle, bordered by the communities of Issaquah, Redmond, and Sammamish in King County. With 22 miles of shoreline and a maximum depth of 105 feet, this natural lake sits in a glacially carved basin surrounded by forested hillsides and upscale residential neighborhoods. Issaquah Creek at the southern end and the Sammamish River at the northern outlet connect the lake to the broader Lake Washington watershed, providing important fish passage corridors.

Smallmouth Bass are the most popular game fish on Lake Sammamish, with rocky points and gravel shorelines holding healthy numbers of bronzebacks that put up spirited fights on light tackle. Yellow Perch are abundant and provide reliable action for shore anglers and families fishing from docks. Cutthroat Trout and stocked Rainbow Trout patrol the lake's deeper, cooler waters and respond well to trolling with small spoons and flies. Kokanee Salmon offer a unique landlocked salmon fishery, with fish available to trollers working the thermocline during summer months.

Lake Sammamish State Park at the southern end of the lake provides the primary public boat launch along with swimming beaches, picnic areas, and trail access. The lake's relatively compact size and protected waters make it an excellent choice for kayak and small-boat anglers who want quality fishing close to the Seattle-Eastside metropolitan area. The combination of accessible launches, diverse fish species, and a beautiful natural setting within an easy drive of over three million residents makes Lake Sammamish one of the most convenient and enjoyable fishing destinations in western Washington.

name: Lake Stevens
description:
Lake Stevens is a popular 1,040-acre lake in Snohomish County that serves as the recreational heart of the growing community that shares its name. With 10 miles of shoreline and a maximum depth of 65 feet, this natural lake sits in a glacially formed basin surrounded by a mix of residential development and wooded shoreline. Its convenient location just east of Interstate 5 between Lake Stevens and Marysville makes it one of the most accessible fishing lakes in the north Puget Sound region.

Largemouth Bass are the primary draw for anglers on Lake Stevens, with healthy weed beds and extensive lily pad coverage providing ideal habitat for bass that regularly reach 3 to 5 pounds. Smallmouth Bass hold along rocky shoreline sections and deeper points, offering a change of pace from the largemouth action. Yellow Perch are plentiful and provide consistent catches for panfish anglers and ice fishermen during the winter months. The Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife stocks Rainbow Trout regularly, and a resident Kokanee population adds a unique salmonid opportunity to this warm-water-dominated fishery.

The city of Lake Stevens maintains public boat launches and waterfront parks that provide excellent access for both motorized and non-motorized boats. The lake's moderate size makes it easy to cover in a single day of fishing, and its relatively calm waters are suitable for anglers in small boats, canoes, and kayaks. The surrounding community offers all necessary services, and the lake's proximity to Marysville and the greater Everett area ensures that visiting anglers will find lodging, dining, and tackle supplies within minutes of the water.

name: Lake Whatcom
description:
Lake Whatcom is a deep, clear 5,000-acre lake that serves as the primary drinking water source for the city of Bellingham and surrounding communities in Whatcom County. Stretching through a forested valley southeast of Bellingham with 26 miles of shoreline and an impressive maximum depth of 318 feet, this three-basin lake is fed by numerous creeks flowing from the western slopes of the Cascade foothills. Its depth, cold water temperatures, and excellent water quality create ideal conditions for both coldwater and warm-water game fish species.

Kokanee Salmon are the signature fishery on Lake Whatcom, with landlocked sockeye providing fast action for trollers using small dodgers and wedding ring spinners from spring through fall. Cutthroat Trout are native to the lake and can be caught by anglers working the shoreline with flies, small spoons, and spinners. Smallmouth and Largemouth Bass offer quality warm-water fishing in the shallower bays and along rocky shoreline structure, while Yellow Perch provide reliable panfish catches throughout the year, including through the ice during cold winters.

Bloedel Donovan Park in Bellingham provides the primary public boat launch on the lake's northern basin, with additional access available at other points around the shoreline. Because the lake serves as Bellingham's water supply, it is subject to certain use restrictions, and anglers should check current regulations before visiting. The lake's proximity to Bellingham, Western Washington University, and the Interstate 5 corridor makes it one of the most convenient quality fishing destinations in the northwestern corner of Washington State.

name: American Lake
description:
American Lake is a 1,124-acre gem located in the city of Lakewood in Pierce County, situated adjacent to Joint Base Lewis-McChord in the south Puget Sound lowlands. With 8 miles of shoreline and a maximum depth of 98 feet, this natural glacial lake features clear water, a diverse bottom structure of sand, gravel, and muck, and a healthy aquatic ecosystem that supports an impressive variety of game fish. Its location within the Tacoma metropolitan area makes it one of the most accessible quality fishing lakes in southern Puget Sound.

The Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife manages American Lake as a multi-species fishery, with regular plants of Rainbow Trout providing reliable action for trollers and shore anglers from fall through spring. Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass offer excellent warm-water fishing throughout the summer months, with fish holding around docks, weed edges, and rocky points. Yellow Perch are abundant and popular with families, while Kokanee provide a unique landlocked salmon fishery. The lake's Tiger Musky program adds trophy potential, with these aggressive hybrids capable of reaching 20 pounds or more and providing heart-stopping strikes for anglers casting large lures and swimbaits.

Harry Todd Park on the lake's western shore provides the primary public boat launch, with a well-maintained ramp, parking, and restroom facilities. The lake's compact size and varied structure make it productive for a wide range of fishing techniques, from bank fishing with PowerBait to trolling with downriggers to casting topwater lures along the shoreline. Its proximity to Lakewood, Tacoma, and the Interstate 5 corridor ensures easy access and nearby services for visiting anglers.

name: Lake Tapps
description:
Lake Tapps is a 2,400-acre reservoir in Pierce County that was originally created in the early 1900s as a power-generation facility and has since evolved into one of the most popular recreational lakes in the south Puget Sound region. With 44 miles of highly irregular shoreline featuring countless coves, points, and islands, and a maximum depth of 65 feet, Lake Tapps offers an extraordinary amount of fishable structure relative to its size. The communities of Bonney Lake, Auburn, and Sumner surround the lake, providing convenient access for anglers throughout the greater Tacoma and south King County areas.

Largemouth Bass are the star attraction at Lake Tapps, with the lake's extensive shoreline development, dock systems, and abundant aquatic vegetation creating ideal bass habitat that produces fish exceeding 5 pounds with regularity. Smallmouth Bass hold along rocky points and gravel transitions, offering excellent catch rates for anglers working deeper structure. Rainbow Trout are stocked by the Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife, and Kokanee provide a fun and tasty salmon option during summer trolling season. Yellow Perch are plentiful in the lake's shallow bays, and the Tiger Musky program introduces an exciting trophy predator that can grow to impressive proportions.

Public boat launches provide access to the lake, with facilities available on both the north and south ends. The lake's 44 miles of shoreline mean that even on busy summer weekends, anglers can find quiet coves and undisturbed fishing spots away from the main recreational areas. Lake Tapps's combination of diverse species, abundant structure, and close proximity to the Puget Sound metropolitan area makes it a favorite among local bass clubs and recreational anglers alike.

name: Columbia River (Washington)
description:
The mighty Columbia River forms much of Washington's southern border with Oregon, flowing 750 miles of shoreline through eight Washington counties on its journey from the inland desert to the Pacific Ocean. As the largest river in the Pacific Northwest and the fourth largest in the United States by volume, the Columbia is a world-class fishing destination that supports legendary runs of salmon, steelhead, and sturgeon. From the Tri-Cities of Kennewick, Richland, and Pasco downstream through the dramatic Columbia River Gorge to the tidal waters near the coast, the Washington side of the Columbia offers unparalleled angling diversity.

Chinook Salmon fishing on the Columbia is the stuff of legend, with spring, summer, and fall runs drawing tens of thousands of anglers to its waters each year. Steelhead, both summer and winter varieties, provide world-class fly and gear fishing throughout the river's length. White Sturgeon, some exceeding 10 feet in length, are targeted on a catch-and-release basis in many sections, offering the chance to battle a true prehistoric giant. Walleye fishing has exploded in popularity behind the river's many dams, with the pools above McNary, John Day, and Bonneville dams producing fish exceeding 10 pounds. Smallmouth Bass, American Shad, and Channel Catfish round out a year-round fishery that always has something in season.

Boat launches are plentiful along the Washington shore, with major facilities in the Tri-Cities, Vancouver, Longview, and numerous smaller communities in between. The Columbia River Gorge section near Stevenson offers some of the most scenic fishing in the state, with towering basalt cliffs and forested mountains framing the river. Whether you are anchoring for Sturgeon below Bonneville Dam, trolling for Chinook in the Tri-Cities reach, or jigging for Walleye in a Columbia pool, the river delivers a fishing experience of truly epic proportions.

name: Snake River (Washington)
description:
The Snake River carves through the deep basalt canyons of southeastern Washington, flowing 140 miles of shoreline through six counties before joining the Columbia River near the Tri-Cities. As the Columbia's largest tributary, the Snake carries vital runs of Steelhead and Chinook Salmon through a rugged landscape of dramatic river canyons, rolling wheat fields, and remote wilderness. The Washington portion of the Snake includes four major dams — Ice Harbor, Lower Monumental, Little Goose, and Lower Granite — creating a series of reservoirs that add warm-water fishing opportunities to the river's outstanding anadromous fishery.

Steelhead fishing on the Snake River is legendary, with both hatchery and wild fish providing challenging and rewarding angling from October through April. Fall Chinook Salmon runs have rebounded strongly in recent years, creating excellent fishing opportunities in the river's lower reaches and reservoir pools. Smallmouth Bass have become a dominant warm-water species in the Snake's reservoirs, with rocky canyon walls and current-washed points holding impressive numbers of hard-fighting bronzebacks. Channel Catfish, Sturgeon, and Walleye provide additional targets throughout the river system.

Clarkston, located at the confluence of the Snake and Clearwater rivers, serves as the primary fishing hub for the upper Snake and gateway to Hells Canyon, the deepest river gorge in North America. Boat launches are available at each of the four dam pools, as well as in Clarkston and at various access points along the river corridor. The communities of Pullman and Dayton provide additional services for visiting anglers exploring this remote and beautiful corner of Washington State.

name: Yakima River
description:
The Yakima River is Washington's premier blue-ribbon trout stream, flowing 215 miles through three counties from its headwaters near Snoqualmie Pass through the Kittitas Valley and Yakima Canyon to its confluence with the Columbia River near Richland. The river's upper reaches between Cle Elum and Ellensburg are nationally recognized for catch-and-release wild Rainbow Trout fishing, with populations exceeding 3,000 fish per mile in some sections. The Yakima Canyon section, where the river winds through a dramatic basalt gorge, is considered one of the finest dry-fly fishing destinations in the western United States.

Wild Rainbow Trout averaging 12 to 16 inches are the Yakima River's signature species, with fish responding eagerly to well-presented dry flies, nymphs, and streamers throughout the year. Prolific hatches of caddisflies, mayflies, and stoneflies create outstanding surface-feeding opportunities from spring through fall. Mountain Whitefish provide additional sport on light tackle, particularly during winter months. The lower river supports runs of Chinook Salmon and Steelhead, while Smallmouth Bass have established populations in the warmer lower reaches below the city of Yakima.

Drift boat fishing is the most popular way to experience the Yakima, with numerous put-in and take-out points between Cle Elum and Roza Dam providing float trips ranging from half a day to multiple days. The communities of Ellensburg, Cle Elum, and Yakima offer guide services, fly shops, lodging, and dining for visiting anglers. The river's accessibility from Interstate 90 and its proximity to Seattle — just two hours over Snoqualmie Pass — make it a convenient weekend escape for Puget Sound anglers seeking world-class trout fishing in a spectacular canyon setting.

name: Skagit River
description:
The Skagit River is the largest river system in the Puget Sound basin and one of only a handful of rivers in the lower 48 states that supports runs of all five species of Pacific salmon — Chinook, Coho, Pink, Chum, and Sockeye. Flowing 150 miles through Skagit and Whatcom counties from its glacial origins on the flanks of the North Cascades to its broad delta on Skagit Bay, the Skagit is a river of national ecological significance. Its watershed encompasses pristine wilderness in North Cascades National Park and Ross Lake National Recreation Area, making it one of the most scenic fishing rivers in the Pacific Northwest.

Steelhead fishing on the Skagit River is world-renowned, drawing fly anglers from across the globe who come to swing traditional Spey flies through the river's classic runs and tailouts from December through April. Wild Skagit Steelhead are prized for their size and power, though current regulations emphasize catch-and-release to protect wild populations. The river's salmon runs bring waves of fish from summer through late fall, with Chinook, Coho, Pink, and Chum Salmon providing varied opportunities throughout the season. Bull Trout and Dolly Varden, both protected char species, inhabit the river's cold upper reaches and tributaries.

Marblemount and Concrete in the upper river valley serve as launching points for drift boat trips through some of the river's most productive and remote water. Mount Vernon and Burlington in the lower valley provide full services and access to the tidal reach and lower river sections. The Skagit's combination of wild salmon and steelhead runs, spectacular mountain scenery, and world-class fly fishing tradition make it Washington's most celebrated river fishery and a must-visit destination for serious Pacific Northwest anglers.
